mechanism: Aspirin inhibits platelet aggregation and may displace Warfarin from protein binding, significantly increasing bleeding risk.
clinical_effect: Major hemorrhage — GI bleeding, intracranial hemorrhage
recommendation: Avoid combination unless specifically indicated by cardiologist (e.g., mechanical valve + recent stent). If co-prescribed, use low-dose Aspirin (75mg) and monitor INR weekly.

mechanism: Tranexamic acid is an antifibrinolytic that promotes clot stability. In anticoagulated patients, this creates opposing pharmacological effects — risk of thromboembolism while on anticoagulation.
clinical_effect: Thromboembolism (stroke, PE, DVT) in anticoagulated patients
recommendation: Avoid unless cardiology explicitly approves. If used for menorrhagia in anticoagulated patient, requires specialist co-management and close INR monitoring.

mechanism: NSAIDs inhibit COX-1, reducing protective gastric prostaglandins and impairing platelet function. Combined with anticoagulation, major bleeding risk increases 3-6 fold.
clinical_effect: GI hemorrhage, prolonged bleeding from any site
recommendation: Avoid all NSAIDs in patients on Warfarin. Use Paracetamol for pain. If NSAID absolutely needed, use lowest dose for shortest duration with PPI cover.

mechanism: Contrast dye can cause acute kidney injury, reducing Metformin clearance and leading to lactic acidosis accumulation.
recommendation: Hold Metformin 48 hours before and after contrast administration. Check renal function before restarting.

mechanism: ACE inhibitors reduce aldosterone secretion, decreasing potassium excretion. Additional potassium supplementation can cause dangerous hyperkalemia.
recommendation: Avoid potassium supplements in patients on ACE inhibitors unless serum potassium is monitored weekly. Never co-prescribe with potassium-sparing diuretics.

mechanism: Dual RAAS blockade causes excessive hypotension, hyperkalemia, and acute kidney injury. No mortality benefit demonstrated.
clinical_effect: Acute kidney injury, hyperkalemia, symptomatic hypotension
recommendation: NEVER combine ACE inhibitor and ARB. Choose one class only.

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal clearance of Methotrexate, leading to toxic accumulation affecting bone marrow, liver, and kidneys.
recommendation: Avoid NSAIDs in patients on Methotrexate. Use Paracetamol for pain. If NSAID unavoidable, hold Methotrexate and monitor CBC/LFT closely.

mechanism: Combined serotonergic activity causes excessive CNS serotonin accumulation.
clinical_effect: Serotonin syndrome — hyperthermia, rigidity, myoclonus, autonomic instability, potentially fatal
recommendation: ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATION. Minimum 2-week washout between stopping one and starting the other (5 weeks for Fluoxetine due to long half-life).

mechanism: NSAIDs cause sodium and water retention, directly opposing the diuretic effect. Also reduce renal prostaglandin-mediated blood flow.
clinical_effect: Reduced antihypertensive/diuretic efficacy, increased risk of acute kidney injury
recommendation: Monitor BP and renal function. Use lowest NSAID dose for shortest duration. Consider Paracetamol as alternative.

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandins; ARBs reduce efferent arteriolar tone. Combined effect significantly reduces glomerular filtration.
clinical_effect: Acute kidney injury, reduced antihypertensive effect
recommendation: Avoid if possible. If used, monitor renal function within 1 week. Ensure adequate hydration.

mechanism: Same as DI_010 — NSAID + ARB combination reduces GFR through complementary mechanisms.
clinical_effect: Acute kidney injury, BP control worsened
recommendation: Avoid. Use Paracetamol instead of Diclofenac when possible.

mechanism: Aspirin may potentiate the hypoglycemic effect of Metformin through uncertain mechanism (possibly enhanced insulin sensitivity).
recommendation: Monitor blood glucose when initiating combination. Usually clinically insignificant at low-dose Aspirin (75mg).

mechanism: Amlodipine inhibits CYP3A4, increasing Simvastatin levels and risk of myopathy.
recommendation: Limit Simvastatin to 20mg/day when co-prescribed with Amlodipine. Consider Atorvastatin or Rosuvastatin as alternatives.

mechanism: Ciprofloxacin inhibits CYP1A2-mediated metabolism of Theophylline, increasing Theophylline levels.
clinical_effect: Theophylline toxicity — nausea, vomiting, seizures, arrhythmia
recommendation: Reduce Theophylline dose by 30-50% or choose Azithromycin instead of Ciprofloxacin.

mechanism: Both independently increase risk of GI ulceration and bleeding. Combined risk is synergistic.
recommendation: Always add PPI cover (Pantoprazole/Omeprazole) when combining. Monitor for GI symptoms.

mechanism: NSAIDs (including COX-2 selective) may enhance the hypoglycemic effect of sulfonylureas through protein binding displacement.
recommendation: Monitor blood glucose when starting NSAID. Warn patient of hypoglycemia symptoms.

mechanism: Venlafaxine (SNRI) has mild noradrenergic effects that can slightly increase blood pressure, partially opposing Amlodipine's antihypertensive effect.
recommendation: Monitor BP at baseline and 2-4 weeks after starting Venlafaxine. Adjust antihypertensive if needed.

mechanism: Both cause CNS depression (Pregabalin) and dizziness. No direct pharmacokinetic interaction.
recommendation: Warn patient about driving and operating machinery. Start Pregabalin at lower dose.

mechanism: Tramadol may enhance insulin secretion through serotonergic mechanism, potentially lowering blood glucose.
recommendation: Monitor blood glucose in diabetic patients receiving Tramadol. Short-term use (peri-operative) is generally safe with monitoring.

mechanism: Tramadol has serotonin reuptake inhibition activity. Combined with SSRIs, increased serotonergic activity.
clinical_effect: Serotonin syndrome (milder than MAOI+SSRI but still clinically significant)
recommendation: Avoid if possible. If needed, use lowest effective doses. Monitor for agitation, tremor, hyperthermia.

mechanism: Calcium binds Levothyroxine in the GI tract, reducing absorption by 20-30%.
clinical_effect: Reduced thyroid hormone levels, hypothyroid symptoms
recommendation: Separate doses by at least 4 hours. Take Levothyroxine on empty stomach in the morning, Calcium with meals later in the day.

mechanism: Iron chelates Levothyroxine in the GI tract, reducing absorption.
recommendation: Separate doses by at least 4 hours.

mechanism: Amoxicillin reduces renal tubular secretion of Methotrexate, increasing Methotrexate blood levels.
recommendation: Monitor CBC if combination unavoidable. Consider Azithromycin as alternative antibiotic.

mechanism: Divalent cations (Al, Mg, Ca, Fe) chelate fluoroquinolones, drastically reducing absorption.
recommendation: Take Ciprofloxacin 2 hours before or 6 hours after antacids.

mechanism: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, which is needed to convert Clopidogrel (prodrug) to its active metabolite.
clinical_effect: Reduced antiplatelet effect of Clopidogrel, increased cardiovascular event risk
recommendation: Use Pantoprazole instead of Omeprazole (less CYP2C19 inhibition). Or use Rabeprazole.

mechanism: Erythromycin inhibits CYP3A4, increasing statin levels.
recommendation: Use Azithromycin instead of Erythromycin. If Erythromycin essential, temporarily hold statin.

mechanism: Amiodarone inhibits P-glycoprotein and renal clearance of Digoxin, doubling Digoxin levels.
clinical_effect: Digoxin toxicity — nausea, visual changes, bradycardia, arrhythmia
recommendation: Reduce Digoxin dose by 50% when starting Amiodarone. Monitor Digoxin levels weekly for first month.

mechanism: NSAIDs reduce renal prostaglandin-mediated lithium clearance, increasing lithium levels by 15-30%.
clinical_effect: Lithium toxicity — tremor, confusion, renal failure, seizures
recommendation: Avoid NSAIDs in patients on Lithium. Use Paracetamol. If NSAID essential, monitor Lithium levels within 5-7 days.

mechanism: Carbamazepine induces CYP3A4, accelerating metabolism of ethinyl estradiol and progestins.
recommendation: Use non-oral contraception (IUD, depot injection) or higher-dose OCP. Counsel patient about reduced efficacy.

mechanism: Both reduce potassium excretion — ARB via RAAS inhibition, Spironolactone as potassium-sparing diuretic.
recommendation: Monitor serum potassium within 1 week of combination and monthly thereafter. Avoid potassium-rich foods.

mechanism: Propranolol (non-selective beta-blocker) blocks beta-2 receptors in bronchial smooth muscle, opposing Salbutamol's bronchodilator effect.
clinical_effect: Bronchospasm, reduced Salbutamol efficacy in asthma
recommendation: AVOID non-selective beta-blockers in asthma patients. Use cardio-selective beta-blocker (Metoprolol, Bisoprolol) if beta-blocker needed.

mechanism: Alcohol inhibits gluconeogenesis and potentiates Metformin's suppression of hepatic glucose output. Also increases lactic acidosis risk.
recommendation: Counsel patients to limit alcohol. Avoid binge drinking. Monitor blood glucose.

mechanism: Both prolong QT interval through different mechanisms (hERG channel blockade).
clinical_effect: QT prolongation → Torsades de Pointes → sudden cardiac death
recommendation: Avoid combination. Use alternative antibiotic (Amoxicillin for respiratory, Cephalexin for skin).

mechanism: Sumatriptan is a 5-HT1B/1D agonist; SSRIs increase serotonin. Theoretical serotonin syndrome risk.
clinical_effect: Serotonin syndrome (rare with triptans, but FDA black box warning exists)
recommendation: Combination is commonly used in practice. Warn patient about symptoms: agitation, tremor, fever. Use lowest effective Sumatriptan dose.

mechanism: Additive sedation — both are antihistamines with CNS depressant effects.
recommendation: Avoid combining. Choose one antihistamine. If Doxylamine used for pregnancy nausea, avoid additional antihistamines.

mechanism: Terbinafine inhibits CYP1A2, reducing caffeine clearance by ~20%.
recommendation: Reduce caffeine intake during Terbinafine course. Generally clinically insignificant.

mechanism: Magnesium trisilicate reduces Nitrofurantoin absorption.
recommendation: Separate doses by 2 hours. Take Nitrofurantoin with food (improves absorption).

mechanism: Ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) may reduce analgesic efficacy of Tramadol, which partly works through serotonergic pathways.
recommendation: Monitor pain control. May need alternative antiemetic (Metoclopramide) or alternative analgesic.

mechanism: Beta-blockers mask hypoglycemia symptoms (tremor, tachycardia) and impair glycogenolysis, prolonging hypoglycemic episodes.
recommendation: Prefer cardio-selective beta-blockers (Metoprolol, Bisoprolol) over non-selective (Propranolol). Counsel patient that sweating is the only reliable hypoglycemia symptom on beta-blockers.

mechanism: Phenytoin induces CYP3A4, increasing metabolism of estrogen and progestin components.
recommendation: Use non-oral contraception (IUD, depot injection) or higher-dose OCP. Same as Carbamazepine interaction.
